item.content:encoded: Consumer Reports Tested 52 Rice Products. These Had the Lowest Arsenic Levels Researchers detected inorganic arsenic in every rice product they tested, underscoring concerns first raised more than a decade ago. By Korin Miller Korin Miller Korin Miller has spent nearly two decades covering food, health, and nutrition for digital, print, and TV platforms. Her work has appeared in Women's Health, SELF, Prevention, The Washington Post, and more. Food & Wine's Editorial Guidelines Published on July 8, 2026 In This Article View All In This Article Consumer Reports found arsenic in every rice product it tested How does arsenic end up in rice? Should you worry about arsenic in rice? How to reduce your exposure to arsenic in rice Rice products with the highest (and lowest) arsenic levels, tested by Consumer Reports Credit: InnaFelker / Getty Images A new Consumer Reports analysis found inorganic arsenic in every rice product it tested, with brown rice averaging higher levels than white rice. Basmati and sushi rice had lower average inorganic arsenic levels than other rice types tested. Rice absorbs more inorganic arsenic than most other grains because it is typically grown in flooded fields, where the element is more readily taken up from soil and water. Experts say the findings aren’t a reason to panic, but choosing lower-arsenic rice varieties, rotating in other grains, and cooking rice in excess water may help reduce exposure to inorganic arsenic. Arsenic has been behind a slew of food recalls over the past few years, from apple juice to supplements. But the highly toxic chemical element has repeatedly surfaced in rice. According to a new Consumer Reports report, the problem isn’t getting better. For the report, researchers tested 52 rice products for arsenic and detected inorganic levels of the compound in all of them. Consumer Reports originally tested rice for arsenic in 2012 and followed up on that testing in 2014. “Consumer Reports has long warned that rice absorbs significantly more inorganic arsenic from soil and water than other crops,” says James Rogers, PhD, Consumer Reports’ director of food safety. Arsenic is a naturally occurring compound found in air, water, and soil, according to the National Cancer Institute (NCI). It can also be released into the environment through agricultural and industrial activities. Arsenic comes in two forms — organic and inorganic. The inorganic form is more toxic and is linked to an increased risk of bladder and skin cancers, among other cancers, according to the NCI. But experts stress that the problem of arsenic in rice is a complicated one. Here’s how concerned you should be about the findings, plus how to lower your risk. Consumer Reports found arsenic in every rice product it tested For the report, researchers analyzed total and inorganic arsenic levels in two to three samples of 52 rice products — arborio, basmati, brown, jasmine, sushi, white, and precooked microwave rice, as well as rice side dishes that use seasoning. The products were also tested for cadmium, lead, and mercury. Based on the findings, the researchers calculated the number of servings adults and children can safely have of each product per week. (Servings varied slightly by product, but typically meant ¼ cup of dry rice.) Billions Eat This Staple Grain Daily, But Climate Change Could Poison the World’s Supply Overall, the researchers found detectable levels of arsenic, cadmium, and mercury in all samples, and lead in 66 samples. Although there is no federal limit for inorganic arsenic in rice, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has set a limit of 100 parts per billion for infant rice cereal. Brown rice consistently contained more inorganic arsenic than white rice, averaging 113 parts per billion compared to an average of 72 parts per billion. “Basmati rice and sushi rice averaged less than other rice types,” Rogers says. Every product had slightly different results, but some of the biggest sources of inorganic arsenic included Ben’s Original Long Grain & Wild Rice (167 parts per billion), Mahatma Jasmine Brown Thai Fragrant Whole Rice (155 PPB), and Goya Organics Long Grain Brown Rice (150 PPB). While brand wasn’t necessarily correlated with high levels of arsenic — some of the brands just mentioned also had rice with lower levels of arsenic — Lundberg consistently had some of the lowest levels of arsenic in its rice products. How does arsenic end up in rice? It’s important to note that companies aren’t adding arsenic to their rice products. Instead, the compound can enter rice during the growing process. “Rice has a unique biology that makes this a difficult problem to solve,” says Scott Keatley, RD, co-owner of Keatley Medical Nutrition Therapy in New York City. “It naturally accumulates arsenic more readily than most other grains because it’s typically grown in flooded fields, where arsenic becomes more available for uptake by the plant.” A New Study Says Up to 17% of the World's Cropland Could Be Contaminated With Toxic Metals Contaminated soil and water allow arsenic to be incorporated into rice plants during the growing process, leading to arsenic contamination of the rice people eat, explains Kelly Johnson-Arbor, MD, a toxicologist at MedStar Health. “In order to significantly reduce this type of contamination, rice growers and processors must change their practices,” she says. “This might include growing rice in drier conditions — which results in less exposure to contaminated water during the growing process — or developing genetically modified strains of rice that are less likely to absorb arsenic from soil or water.” But these types of interventions take years to develop, research, and implement, which could explain why arsenic contamination in rice hasn’t declined over the last decade, Johnson-Arbor says. Should you worry about arsenic in rice? The primary concern is long-term exposure, according to Jamie Alan, PhD, an associate professor of pharmacology and toxicology at Michigan State University. “This is inorganic arsenic, which compared to organic arsenic, is much more toxic,” she says. It’s difficult to know exactly how concerning the findings are, given that there is no upper limit on arsenic exposure for adults and children, she says. “Generally, adults and children can tolerate more of external toxins like arsenic than infants, so I would expect that these groups could safely consume more,” Alan says. “However, I cannot be sure because the data does not exist.” But Johnson-Arbor says that a lot depends on your overall diet. “Plant-based foods can contain detectable levels of heavy metals, including arsenic, cadmium, and lead,” she says. “However, for people who eat a balanced diet that contains various types of foods, it is unlikely that such exposures will result in toxicity or unwanted health effects.” How to reduce your exposure to arsenic in rice Levels of inorganic arsenic in rice depend in part on where it is grown. “Studies have generally found lower inorganic arsenic levels in rice grown in places like California, India, Pakistan, and Thailand, while higher levels are more common in rice from parts of the south-central United States — particularly Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, and Texas — because flooded growing conditions and local soil chemistry increase arsenic uptake,” Keatley says. “For the average person, that means the risk depends not just on how much rice you eat, but also where it was grown.” Keatley also recommends rotating other whole grains into your diet beyond rice, such as quinoa, barley, farro, oats, and sorghum, to reduce cumulative exposure while maintaining nutritional variety. “When possible, choose lower-arsenic varieties like basmati, and consider cooking rice in excess water and draining it afterward, which can substantially reduce its inorganic arsenic content,” he says. Consumer Reports tested 52 rice products and found detectable inorganic arsenic in every sample, echoing concerns first raised more than a decade ago. The toxic form of arsenic has been linked to cancer with long-term exposure. sakai000 / Getty Images If you’re concerned about arsenic in rice, Johnson-Arbor suggests limiting your rice intake to a few servings a week and avoiding prepackaged rice with spices, which tends to have consistently higher arsenic levels. Overall, Alan says the findings aren’t a reason to panic, but they do suggest people should be mindful of the type of rice they eat. “If you can switch your brand of rice to one with lower levels, then do so,” she says. “Our bodies are equipped to handle a certain amount of external toxins. If you have rice once or twice a week, you are completely fine.” Rice products with the highest (and lowest) arsenic levels, tested by Consumer Reports The amount of inorganic arsenic varied widely depending on the type of rice and the specific product tested. Brown rice generally had higher arsenic levels than white rice, while basmati and sushi rice tended to have lower levels overall. Consumer Reports also calculated how many servings per week adults and children could safely eat of each product based on its findings. The tables below break down the arsenic levels and the recommended weekly serving limits for every rice product included in the testing. Use them to compare brands and identify products that may help reduce your overall arsenic exposure. item.title: Japan does not cut down centuries-old trees for development. Instead, experts spend months preparing their roots and relocating them
item.description: The TOI Science Desk stands as an inquisitive team of journalists, ceaselessly delving into the realms of discovery to curate a captivating collection of news,…
item.content:encoded: Sign In News Videos India World City Gadgets Now Life & Style Entertainment Business Tech TOI Games Cricket Sports TV Web Series Education Speaking Tree Relationships TOI Newsletters Health Real Estate Legal Defence Women Live Japan does not cut down centuries-old trees for development. Instead, experts spend months preparing their roots and relocating them TOI Science Desk / TIMESOFINDIA.COM / Updated: Jul 11, 2026, 12:26 IST When roads, railways or new buildings are planned, centuries-old trees are often among the first casualties of development. In Japan, however, some of the country's most valuable trees are given a second chance. Rather than cutting them down, arborists and horticultural experts sometimes relocate them using a painstaking process that can take months or even more than a year. Before a tree is moved, specialists carefully prepare its root system to improve its chances of surviving in a new location. The technique, known as nemawashi, combines centuries of horticultural knowledge with modern engineering and reflects Japan's long-standing respect for nature and cultural heritage. Its continued use highlights a conservation philosophy that values preserving living history, proving that development and environmental stewardship can sometimes exist side by side. Why Japan relocates some centuries-old trees instead of cutting them down Not every tree standing in the path of development is relocated. The practice is generally reserved for trees with exceptional historical, cultural or ecological value. Many of these trees are found in temple grounds, shrines, traditional gardens, parks or historic neighbourhoods, where they have stood for generations and are considered part of the local heritage. In some cases, moving a mature tree also helps preserve biodiversity and the character of a landscape while allowing infrastructure projects to continue. Because the process is expensive and technically demanding, authorities carefully evaluate whether relocation is practical before deciding against removal. The months-long preparation hidden beneath the ground The most remarkable part of the relocation process happens long before cranes arrive. Months before transplantation, specialists begin carefully pruning the tree's roots. Instead of uprooting the entire root system at once, they gradually trim selected roots around the tree. This encourages the growth of new, fine feeder roots closer to the trunk. These feeder roots are essential because they absorb most of the water and nutrients needed for survival. By allowing them to develop before relocation, experts significantly reduce transplant shock and improve the tree's chances of adapting to its new environment. Depending on the size and species, this preparation may take anywhere from six months to over a year. What is nemawashi and why does it matter? The root preparation process is known in Japan as nemawashi, a term that literally translates to "going around the roots." Originally a horticultural practice, nemawashi has become a widely used expression in Japanese business and politics, where it refers to quietly laying the groundwork before introducing major changes. The metaphor comes directly from tree transplantation: careful preparation before taking decisive action leads to better outcomes. In gardening, the principle remains the same. Preparing the roots first greatly increases the likelihood that a mature tree will survive the move. How Japan moves giant trees Once the root system is ready, the relocation begins. Workers excavate around the tree to preserve as much of the prepared root ball as possible. The roots are wrapped in protective materials such as burlap, while the trunk and branches may also be secured to prevent damage during transport. Depending on the tree's size, cranes, hydraulic lifting equipment or specially designed transport vehicles are used to move it to its new location. There, it is replanted in a carefully prepared pit, supported with stabilising structures and monitored closely through regular watering and maintenance until it establishes new growth. Does every relocated tree survive? Even with months of preparation, relocating a mature tree is never guaranteed to succeed. Larger trees experience significant stress because part of their root system is inevitably lost during transplantation. Success depends on factors including the species, age, health of the tree, timing of the move and the quality of aftercare. For this reason, Japanese arborists reserve the technique for trees whose cultural, historical or environmental value justifies the considerable cost and effort involved. A lesson in balancing development and conservation Japan's approach demonstrates that urban development and environmental conservation do not always have to be opposing goals. Although relocating centuries-old trees requires careful planning, specialised expertise and significant investment, it allows some of the country's most treasured natural landmarks to survive for future generations. Rather than viewing ancient trees as obstacles, the practice treats them as living heritage worthy of preservation, even as cities continue to grow. A horticultural practice so precise and patient that it became a metaphor for how to introduce change carefully in business and politics is carrying more meaning than most agricultural techniques manage. Nemawashi works on trees because you cannot rush root development, and it works as a concept in organisations for exactly the same reason: the preparation is the work, not a delay before the work begins. About the Author TOI Science Desk The TOI Science Desk stands as an inquisitive team of journalists, ceaselessly delving into the realms of discovery to curate a captivating collection of news, features, and articles from the vast and ever-evolving world of science for the readers of The Times of India. Consider us your scientific companion, delivering a daily dose of wonder and enlightenment. Whether it's the intricacies of genetic engineering, the marvels of space exploration, or the latest in artificial intelligence, the TOI Science Desk ensures you stay connected to the pulse of the scientific world. At the TOI Science Desk, we are not just reporters; we are storytellers of scientific narratives. We are committed to demystifying the intricacies of science, making it accessible and engaging for readers of all backgrounds. Join us as we craft knowledge with precision and passion, bringing you on a journey where the mysteries of the universe unfold with every word.Read More End of Article Follow Us On Social Media Photostories From the Amazon to India: 10 of the largest snakes you'll ever encounter How to know if fish is fresh: 5 simple signs that can save you from buying stale seafood Have you visited the world's smallest country yet? 5 things to know before planning a visit What happens to your body when you eat chickpeas every day for a month From Samosa to Bread Pakoda: Popular deep-fried snacks of India and their calorie count you should know Top 6 most visited national parks in the USA every nature lover should experience once in their lifetime Kidney damage: Doctor says these 9 silent symptoms may not seem kidney-related but should never be ignored 10 baby names that mean "miracle" in different cultures 6 stunning red birds in India and where to find them Sreeleela and Kalyani Priyadarshan ace the Wimbledon dress code with timeless London fashion Hot Picks SIR Blake Lively Gurgaon Encounter CM Vijay Alex Pereira PM Modi Nico Hischier Strait of Hormuz Simone Biles Explore Every Corner Across The Globe Mikel MerinoHyderabad Murder CaseSheikh HasinaShubman GillSpain vs BelgiumNarottam MishraPune Building CollapseRohit ChandelPrabhsimran SinghChembur School Bus TragedyIran warPaige BueckersMP Ethanol ScamTrump Assassination PlotDeepak NandalBankipur Assembly BypollGurgaon Shooting IncidentSpain WildfireDelhi Red Fort Bomb ThreatTrump warns IranChristopher Blake GriffithBishnoi Gang Member ArrestedWest Bengal UCC BillRam Temple Donation TheftIndia Vs England WIndia Supply GasolineAnn WiddecombeKing PhilippeSai MarthandAnaheim Ducks News Science News Japan does not cut down centuries-old trees for development. item.content:encoded: Our Most Popular Summer Casserole Recipe of All Time Fresh corn, browned butter, and plenty of cheese make this side dish a perennial favorite. By Leah Colins Leah Colins Leah is the Senior Culinary Editor at Serious Eats, and was previously a recipe developer and editor with America's Test Kitchen for almost 9 years. She has developed recipes for and edited over 20 cookbooks ranging in topic from bread baking to plant-based eating to outdoor grilling and so much more. While there, she also developed recipes and articles for Cooks Illustrated Magazine, Cooks Country Magazine, and ATK's digital platform.Before her life as a recipe developer, she cooked in 5-star and Michelin-starred fine dining establishments from coast to coast such as The Herbfarm and Aubergine Restaurant at L'Auberge Carmel; she also treasures her time flipping burgers on flattops in her teenage years, and baking and boxing cookies and pies at a wonderful family-owned German bakery in her early professional life. Serious Eats' Editorial Guidelines Published on July 9, 2026 Credit: Serious Eats / Morgan Hunt Glaze Mention a casserole, and most of us picture bubbling baked pasta, creamy potato gratins, or cozy weeknight dinners that belong firmly in sweater weather. But there’s no reason casseroles should disappear once summer arrives. In fact, a casserole is one of the easiest ways to turn peak-season produce into a low-effort meal or side dish that comes together in a single pan and feeds a crowd. They’re perfect for family dinners, as well as summer entertaining. And if our readers have anything to say about it, one summer casserole stands above the rest: Stella Parks‘ corn casserole. Corn casserole can mean different things to different cooks. Sometimes it’s little more than canned corn mixed with boxed corn muffin mix. Elsewhere, it’s closer to a custardy corn pudding rich with cream and eggs. Stella’s version is comfortably in between. It’s hearty without being heavy, creamy, and packed with enough fresh corn flavor to remind you why you’re making it in July in the first place. Her recipe takes a more thoughtful approach than simply stirring everything together and hoping for the best. The recipe begins with sautéing onion, red bell pepper, and fresh sage in butter until the vegetables soften and the butter begins to brown, building layers of savory, nutty flavor before the corn enters the pan. Once that flavorful base is built, the kernels go in. And while you might expect frozen corn to be the default outside of peak season, Stella actually recommends fresh corn year-round—even when it’s past its prime. While older corn might not be much fun to eat off the cob, she explains that its starchier texture actually works in the casserole’s favor, helping give the finished dish more body. (If fresh isn’t available, though, frozen corn works just fine.) After the kernels cook just long enough to shed some of their moisture, a modest amount of cornmeal gives the casserole body and structure without turning it into cornbread. Eggs, milk, and cream create a silky custard that binds everything together while the sweet kernels remain the focus. The entire dish comes together in one skillet, because summer is hot enough without adding a mountain of dishes to the day’s to-do list. A generous layer of cheese—sharp cheddar, Monterey Jack, or Stella’s favorite, Parmesan—melts into a savory topping that bubbles under the broiler. While it’s tempting to dig in immediately, the casserole improves as it rests. As it cools slightly, the eggs and cornmeal finish setting, ensuring neat, creamy slices. This casserole is equally at home next to barbecue, fried chicken, grilled steaks, or a simple tomato salad. And judging by how often readers come back to it every corn season, it’s a reminder that some of the best casseroles are the ones you make in July, not January. Sweet and Savory Corn Casserole Read More: Features Personal Essays Was this page helpful? Thanks for your feedback! Tell us why! Other Submit

item.content:encoded: Pyrex And Anchor Hocking Look Similar, But They’re Not The Same—Here’s Why A professional chef breaks down the differences between these beloved glassware brands. By Kait Hanson Kait Hanson Kait Hanson is a lifestyle reporter with more than ten years of experience writing stories on entertainment, travel, food and more. Her work has been featured by Vanity Fair, CNN, TIME, TODAY and more. Southern Living's editorial guidelines Published on July 09, 2026 In This Article View All In This Article Similarities Types Of Glass Pyrex Anchor Hocking Which Is Better? When trying to pick out quality glass cookware for your kitchen, brands like Pyrex and Anchor Hocking may be top of mind. Both brands have made a name for themselves in the durable glass cookware market and come with glowing reviews from loyal fans. It begs the question: What is the difference between Pyrex vs. Anchor Hocking? How do the two brands compare? Francine Marz, a South Carolina chef, breaks it down for us. Credit: Caitlin Bensel What’s Similar About Pyrex Vs. Anchor Hocking? Marz tells Southern Living the brands are similar in what they create: glass cookware, bakeware, and kitchen accessories. “Both are made of the same kind of materials, both companies are over 100 years old, both offer limited warranties on their products, and both companies offer a wide variety of items,” she says. “Both are BPA-free and depending on your stance, that could equate to another positive attribute of using glass cooking (and) baking vessels.” Plus, both brands create products using soda-lime glass and can be put into an oven up to 425 degrees Fahrenheit. Y’all Don’t Want To Miss This Join Southern Living Insiders today to unlock 60 years of vintage recipes, exclusive deals, a special edition of our best-selling cookbook, and a weekly newsletter just for Insiders. Types Of Glass Oven-safe cookware like Pyrex and Anchor Hocking are made with unique glass that’s resistant to heat, chemicals, and electricity. They are safe to use in the oven and the freezer. There are three types of this glassware: soda-lime, borosilicate, and tempered. Borosilicate glass is made of boron oxide and silica. As cookware, it is extremely durable, and it is also used in making glass laboratory supplies. Soda-lime glass is made from a mixture of soda, lime, and silica. It has a lower resistance to heat and chemicals than borosilicate glass. Tempering makes glass stronger and more resistant to thermal shock. What Is Pyrex? Founded in New York in 1915, Pyrex was born in New York when Bessie Littleton was seeking a solution to a cracked baking dish. At the time, her husband was working for Corning Glass Works and provided Bessie with a sawed-off glass jar to test for baking. It worked—and the rest is history. Pyrex produces glass bakeware and cookware, glass storage containers, and the iconic glass measuring cups many of us have in our own kitchens. Early Pyrex was made from borosilicate, which could withstand extreme temperature changes, or thermal shock, without shattering. The company switched to tempered soda-lime glass in the 1950s to keep the cost affordable for consumers while still providing glassware tough enough for everyday cooking. It is stronger than borosilicate, making it less likely to shatter when dropped. It is also less heat resistant than vintage Pyrex made with borosilicate. Vintage Pyrex is marked as “PYREX,” in all capital letters. Today, the company’s glassware is labeled as “pyrex.” What Is Anchor Hocking? Anchor Hocking was founded in 1905 by Isaac Jacob Collins in Ohio. Collins’ goal was simple: to make glass bakeware that was accessible. More than 100 years later, the brand has evolved from glass bakeware into other glass products that include food storage, serveware, drinkware, mixing bowls, and more. Anchor Hocking glass bakeware is made with tempered soda-lime glass, giving it durability and heat resistance. Like Pyrex, it was originally made with borosilicate glass. Is Glass Good For Baking? Both brands produce glass bakeware and cookware, but is glass the best option when it comes to cooking and baking? Marz says glassware in the kitchen is beloved because it typically bakes evenly every time. Glassware retains heat to keep food warm longer, it doesn’t stain, and you can bake, serve, and store all in one dish. “I think it’s fabulous, but it is heavy and it sometimes can be hard to carry around or move,” she says. “It doesn’t rust like metal and it’s easy to clean; however, you have to be careful to make sure that you don’t expose the bakeware to sudden extreme heat changes.” How To Safely Use Pyrex And Anchor Hocking Soda-lime glass, which is used to make both Pyrex and Anchor Hocking glassware, is safe to use in the oven and the freezer. But there are a few guidelines to prevent shattering. Avoid Extreme Temperatures Soda-lime glassware is less thermal resistant than earlier versions made with borosilicate, so don’t subject it to extreme temperatures, such as going straight from the freezer to the hot oven. Bring a casserole frozen in a Pyrex or Anchor Hocking dish to room temperature before putting it in the oven. And a hot dish should be allowed to cool completely before storing it in the freezer. Don’t Use The Cooktop This type of glassware is not suitable for stovetop cooking. You also shouldn’t set it on a hot cooktop when placing or removing it from the oven. Let It Cool When removing glass bakeware from the oven, let it cool on a wire baking rack instead of on a cool surface like the counter. Make sure the surface is dry and not wet to prevent cracking. A hot glass dish should also cool before washing or storing in the refrigerator or freezer. Check For Chips Periodically check your glass cookware for chips and cracks. These can weaken the dish and make it more prone to breakage in a hot oven or due to changes in temperature. Make Sure It’s Oven-Safe Before putting glass baking dishes into the oven, microwave, freezer, or dishwasher, check the bottom of the dish or contact the manufacturer to see if it’s safe to do so. Which Is Better: Pyrex Or Anchor Hocking? So, which brand takes gold? Marz says that answer is subjective. “I really think it is personal preference and we tend to use what our parents used in the kitchen,” Marz says. “The rituals of baking and cooking stimulate memories, feelings, and emotions, and something as simple as a glass baking dish might not mean anything to one person, but could mean so much to another. Today there are so many knockoff brands that one has to truly be loyal to a brand to keep purchasing it.” Explore more: Food and Recipes Kitchen Assistant Was this page helpful? Thanks for your feedback! Tell us why! Other Submit

item.title: On July 7, a small Miami company called City Labs put a tritium-powered battery the size of a pencil eraser into orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 — its softball-sized BOHR CubeSat becoming the first commercial spacecraft ever cleared to carry a nuclear power source under the FAA pathway built after a 2019 White House memo
item.description: Early Tuesday morning, a softball-sized satellite built by Miami’s City Labs reached low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9, becoming the first commercially…
item.content:encoded: Early Tuesday morning, a softball-sized satellite built by Miami’s City Labs reached low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9, becoming the first commercially built spacecraft to carry a nuclear power source and the first commercial nuclear mission cleared under the Federal Aviation Administration’s new launch approval process. The BOHR satellite — short for Betavoltaic Orbital High-Reliability — flew as one of 81 payloads on SpaceX’s Transporter-17 rideshare mission from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California. It settled into low Earth orbit carrying a tritium-powered battery roughly the size of a pencil eraser that City Labs hopes will open a regulatory and technical door for commercial nuclear spacecraft. City Labs CEO Peter Cabauy described the launch as a historic step for commercial nuclear power in space. According to City Labs, BOHR demonstrates the readiness of safe, compact, regulatory-approved nuclear power systems for routine commercial deployment. What actually flew BOHR is a 1U CubeSat — the smallest standard size in the modular satellite format — and its everyday operations run on solar panels. The nuclear component is a payload, not the primary bus power. City Labs is treating this flight as a technology demonstration, not an operational nuclear satellite in the way that phrase would apply to, say, a Voyager probe. The power source is a betavoltaic battery. Rather than using heat from radioactive decay, as the plutonium-fueled radioisotope thermoelectric generators on NASA’s deep-space probes do, a betavoltaic device converts beta particles emitted by decaying tritium directly into electricity using a semiconductor. The output is measured in nanowatts to microwatts. That is a small number. It is enough to keep a low-power sensor alive or a security key alive for years, but nowhere near enough to run a communications satellite, let alone a lunar habitat. Why tritium, and why now Tritium is a radioactive isotope of hydrogen with a half-life of about 12 years. Its emissions are weak. The Nuclear Regulatory Commission describes the radiation as a low-energy beta particle similar to an electron that cannot penetrate skin and does not travel far in air. That property is what makes commercial launch approval plausible in the first place. The batteries are safe enough to ship to a residential address, a distinction from the usual handling regime for nuclear materials, which typically move only between facilities licensed to handle radiation. The tritium in BOHR’s battery is expected to keep producing usable current for roughly a decade, though City Labs expects to have flight data on the payload’s performance within weeks to months. Related: Writing a single 100-word email with ChatGPT consumes approximately the volume of a standard bottle of water, the global infrastructure processing AI queries is projected to use the equivalent of half the United Kingdom’s annual water withdrawal by 2027, and much of that water is being drawn from regions already experiencing severe drought. The regulatory pathway matters more than the wattage For the commercial space industry, the significance of BOHR is less about the electricity it produces and more about the paperwork it survived. It is the first commercial nuclear payload authorized under the FAA’s launch approval process implemented following National Security Presidential Memorandum-20, a policy signed on August 20, 2019 that restructured how the U.S. government reviews launches of spacecraft carrying nuclear material. Before this pathway existed, any nuclear payload effectively required a case-by-case interagency review at the highest levels of government. That worked for the handful of NASA science missions carrying plutonium-238, but it did not scale for a commercial industry that might eventually want to fly hundreds of small nuclear payloads on rideshare missions. The FAA issued its affirmative payload authorization for BOHR on September 30, 2025, following a safety analysis led by City Labs’ Kevin Makinson and independently validated by Sandia National Laboratories. That authorization is now a template. Other companies working on radioisotope power for small spacecraft — and there are several — now have a documented example of how the review works, what the FAA required, and what the timeline looks like. For a sector that has spent years arguing that regulatory uncertainty is the biggest obstacle to commercial nuclear power, that is real progress. The gap between BOHR and a Moon base Cabauy is candid about the ceiling on betavoltaic technology as it exists today. Nanowatts to microwatts will not run a rover. It will not run a radio strong enough to reach Earth from the lunar south pole. It will not keep astronauts warm through a 14-day lunar night. What it can do is keep a sensor node alive in a permanently shadowed crater — the kind of environment where solar panels are useless and chemical batteries freeze. NASA has been working with City Labs on exactly that application, exploring tritium power sources for instruments in the shadowed regions near the Moon’s poles that are of increasing interest for water ice extraction. The company is also developing heat-producing versions of the technology, which Cabauy said can generate tens of watts of thermal output — closer to what small lunar surface missions actually need. That figure is still an order of magnitude short of what a crewed base would draw, which is why NASA is separately funding development of full fission surface reactors for the Moon, targeted for deployment by 2030. National security is the quiet customer BOHR was funded through a Department of Defense contract, and City Labs holds additional research awards from both the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and SpaceWERX. City Labs indicated the mission could serve as a pathfinder for future nuclear-powered spacecraft in both civil and national security applications. The military interest is straightforward. Low-power, long-life batteries that do not depend on sunlight are useful for classified sensor payloads, secure communications nodes, and any spacecraft that might need to operate in shadowed orbits or drift for years without maintenance. A power source with a decade-long half-life and no moving parts is attractive to mission planners who value reliability above all else. That dual-use character — civilian science and military utility riding the same regulatory approval — is a familiar pattern in space policy. It is also part of why the FAA pathway matters. Commercial nuclear approval built for a small Miami startup can also be used by a large defense contractor. What to watch next City Labs says the tritium payload will report performance data within weeks. That data will determine whether the betavoltaic device behaves in orbit the way it does in the lab — surviving launch vibration, thermal cycling, and the radiation environment of low Earth orbit without measurable degradation. Lockheed Martin ran environmental tests on the company’s tritium devices beginning in 2008, and those units are reportedly still functioning, but ground testing is not orbital operation. The broader question is whether other companies follow. If a second commercial nuclear payload flies within the next year or two under the same FAA process, BOHR will have done what its builders hoped: turned a one-off approval into a routine one. If it remains the only example, the flight will still stand as a technical first, but the commercial nuclear era will remain theoretical. Either way, a softball-sized satellite carrying a battery smaller than a paper clip has now flown as the leading edge of a category the space industry has been talking about for decades. The wattage is trivial. The precedent is not.
